title: Analytic Origin of Green-Function Compression in the Intermediate Representation
abstract: Information compression plays a central role in diverse fields of modern science and technology, from communication theory to machine learning. In condensed-matter physics, the intermediate representation (IR) basis has recently been developed as an efficient method for compressing imaginary-time Green functions, which are fundamental quantities for describing quantum many-body systems. This compression relies on the rapid decay of the singular values with the basis index and the unusually weak growth of the effective rank with inverse temperature. Because of these useful features, the IR basis is now widely used as a standard method in quantum many-body calculations. However, the analytic origin of its compression capability has remained unclear. Here we uncover a finite-Laplace-transform structure underlying the IR kernel, which reveals that the eigenfunctions of the IR kernel admit a natural expansion in terms of classical special functions, the oblate spheroidal wave functions. This finite-Laplace-transform structure also enables us to analytically clarify the compression mechanism of the IR basis. Our results provide a mathematical foundation for the compression of imaginary-time Green functions, connecting quantum many-body physics with theories of information compression and finite integral transforms.
summary: This paper provides the first analytic explanation for why the Intermediate Representation (IR) basis is so efficient at compressing imaginary-time Green functions. By identifying a finite-Laplace-transform structure, the author shows that the basis is linked to oblate spheroidal wave functions. This explains the logarithmic growth of the effective rank for fermions and its saturation for bosons.
main_problem: The paper seeks to provide an analytic explanation for the compression capability of the Intermediate Representation (IR) basis used in many-body physics, specifically why its effective rank grows only logarithmically with inverse temperature for fermions and saturates for bosons.
main_result: The author reveals that the IR kernel possesses a finite-Laplace-transform structure, allowing IR eigenfunctions to be expanded in terms of oblate spheroidal wave functions, which analytically explains the rapid decay of singular values and the specific scaling of the effective rank.
method: The study employs Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of imaginary-time-frequency kernels, reduces the problem to a generalized eigenproblem via a finite Laplace transform, and uses Sturm-Liouville operator theory to map the problem to oblate spheroidal wave functions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on quantum many-body systems described by Matsubara Green functions for both fermionic and bosonic statistics in the imaginary-time domain.
key_observables: Singular values (s_l), effective rank (N_eff), and the IR kernel decay rate.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), frequency cutoff (omega_max), dimensionless parameter (Lambda = beta * omega_max), and the threshold (epsilon) for singular value truncation.
main_assumptions: The mathematical derivation assumes smooth functions and test functions, and that boundary terms vanish during integration by parts.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares direct SVD with the new spheroidal expansion and shows the decay of expansion coefficients. Figure 2 shows the agreement between predicted and actual singular value decay and demonstrates the logarithmic scaling of the effective rank with Lambda.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of IR compression, develops a mathematical framework reducing the IR kernel to a finite Laplace transform, demonstrates the connection to oblate spheroidal wave functions, provides asymptotic scaling analysis for fermionic and bosonic regimes, and includes appendices for the formal proofs of operator commutation and reconstruction formulas.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for a widely used numerical technique in many-body simulations, connecting the efficiency of Green's function compression to classical problems in signal processing and special functions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
